frame: Religious and Caste Identity as Dress Mandate: Her Body as Community Property
generated_comment: Maa, you won't believe what happened today at the function. Just imagine, I walked in, and before I could even greet anyone properly, Mummyji, right in front of all the relatives, she looks at me head to toe and says, 'Meera, did you forget you're a bahu of this house, not a college girl in Pune? This isn't how our daughters-in-law present themselves.' She literally said that, Maa! In front of everyone! And I was wearing a perfectly good salwar kameez, a new one! Not even anything revealing. And the worst part? [Husband's name] was standing right there! He didn't say a word. Not a single word to defend me. He just looked down. I felt... I felt like my body wasn't my own anymore, Maa. Like I was just a display piece for *their* customs. It's like slowly, piece by piece, they're chipping away at who I am. I don't even recognize myself in the mirror anymore sometimes. Is this what being married means? Losing everything that makes you, you?
label: non_misogynistic
annotation_note: This dialogue describes the character's reaction to experiencing misogyny rather than expressing it herself. The mother-in-law's original comment (recounted by Meera) embodies the frame where Meera's body is treated as community property, subjected to dress mandates based on religious and caste identity. Meera's dialogue expresses her frustration and loss of self-identity due to these expectations and her husband's inaction.
persona_text: You are Meera. Age 27. Married. A woman from a Brahmin family in Pune who married a man from a Kshatriya family in Rajasthan. The two families have different traditional dress norms for women. Your husband's family expects you to wear a saree and cover your head at family functions. Your own family never required this. You are on a phone call with your mother after a particularly difficult family function at your in-laws' house where your mother in law made a comment in front of everyone about how you were dressed. You are telling your mother what was said and how you felt and what your husband did and did not do and what you think is happening to your sense of yourself inside this marriage.
